speaker: interviewer
text: Are you ready? OK. All right. Who is this? And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1949. The end of 1949, in Warsaw, Poland. Me and mine future wife. That's when she was my girlfriend, Szyfra.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, again, is me and my wife, taken in Jerusalem, in 1977. Jerusalem marketplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at the Hyatt Regency Hotel in Houston, Texas, 12 years ago, then Yitzhak Rabin-- Prime Minister of Israel, killed three, two months ago-- was at a visit. And he invited me to come in. He used to be my Commanding Officer in Israeli Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yitzhak Rabin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my two youngest grandsons. Clayton, five, and Gavriel, the little guy, number two. Two years old. [LAUGHS] This is a family picture taken at the bar mitzvah of mine second grandson, which is Lonn Weissblum. He is now 20 years old. And this was at his bar mitzvah, 13-year-old. It was taken in city of New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: So go back six years. So it's around 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's start over here. And you can tell me who each of these people is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joshua, my grandson. Jacob, my grandson. Randy, my son-in-law. Dina, my daughter. John, my son-in-law. Sylvia, my daughter. Faye, my daughter. Lonn, grandson. Ken Weissblum, son-in-law. That's me, my wife, Michelle, Karen, my daughter-in-law, my son. And Danieli, my daughter-- my granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] OK. All righty.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1944, in the city Krasnik, Poland, after liberation. It's me here, [? Oizer ?] lives in California. Chaim fell on the way to Jerusalem, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell their names for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Oizer ?] [? Rosenberg, ?] Chaim [? Lipschitz. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Driver's License, issued in Israel in August, 1956. And this was my first license for truck driving. This picture was taken in 1944, in the city of Krasnik, after liberation. It's me, my wife, and one of her girlfriends, which I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but she wasn't your wife yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We just knew each other as friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken on my farm in Israel, when I came back on leave from the army. The black beret represented the Tank Division.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken in 1973, at mine son's bar mitzvah. I'm in the middle. To the right is my younger sister, Esther. To the left is my older sister, Regina.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken last week in Florida. I took it myself. It's my mother. She's 97. Till 120 years old. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is Nathan Aptekar's addition to his interview. February 29, 1996. Mr. Aptekar, now, tell us what year this story took place.

speaker: interviewee
text: This took place in 1942, our first hiding place by the family Matisek. We were still in the attic, before the bunker was built. The Polish volunteer firemen caught one of our known friends. Shoemaker in town-- his name was Meyer Risner.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that? Risner, R-I-S-N-E-R. The volunteers delivered him to the Germans. The Germans promised him that if he will disclose where other Jews are hiding, they will let them go free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took them to our hiding place. I used to play, always, with his kids. And he liked me very much. Maybe that was the reason he had a change of heart. But 20 feet before the building, before bringing in to us, he decided to escape. And they shot him dead. After they shot him, they figured out he mislead them. And they did not go-- come in to look for us. If they would have come in, they would easy find us. We were in the attic. This shoemaker was buried next to the bridge in town, where the water passes by every spring, when the snow melts. Excuse me. Twice, his body was washed out. And then the Polish people from town decided that the Jews are buried standing, not laying down, for him not to be bashed out. And they buried him standing. When I was there a year ago with my Polish friend from Houston, they heard the story from the Polish people over there. And the Polish friends, well, they went from me from Houston, is Elizabeth and Andrew Pollak, P-O-L-L-A-K, spells. They live in 10935 Kitty Brook, Houston Texas. They left money to Polish friends to light a candle every year in the memory of the shoemaker. But he is still buried standing up in the town of Zdzilowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: All got killed. All of them from the woods, and were Polish people. None of them was killed by bullet. Only hacked to death. His youngest son was tied to a tree, and with the-- like a baseball bat, smashed his head to pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoa.
